summary: Paul contrasts human eloquence with Spirit-revealed wisdom, showing that Christ crucified is understood through the mind of Christ.

outline.title: Christ Crucified, Not Eloquence
outline.summary: Paul recalls proclaiming the cross in weakness so that Corinthian faith would rest on God’s power.

outline.title: God’s Wisdom Revealed
outline.summary: God’s hidden saving purpose surpasses worldly wisdom and is made known through the Spirit.

outline.title: The Spirit and Christ’s Mind
outline.summary: The Spirit enables believers to receive, understand, and speak the things freely given by God.

verses.bibleText: And I, brethren, when I came to you, came not with excellency of speech or of wisdom, declaring unto you the testimony of God.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul recalls his first arrival in Corinth. He did not present God’s testimony with the cultivated display expected from a traveling lecturer or public orator. The point is not that his sentences were confused or his reasoning careless. Acts portrays him arguing in the synagogue and persuading hearers. What he renounced was speech designed to make the messenger, rather than the message, the object of admiration.

The expression “excellency of speech” can describe elevated or superior discourse. Paul’s refusal should be read alongside the substance of his letter: he uses irony, analogy, quotation, and tightly connected reasoning. He has not abandoned rhetoric in the broad sense; he has refused the conventions by which a speaker advertised social distinction.

Corinth stood within a culture of public performance where trained speakers could attract pupils and patrons. A new congregation tempted to compare Paul with the more eloquent Apollos needed to remember how it had actually begun. The founder arrived as a working missionary bearing testimony, not as a professional sage gathering a school around his person.

The phrase “testimony of God” names something entrusted to him. Paul came as a witness, not an inventor selling a private philosophy. That posture suited the gospel’s content: a crucified Messiah cannot be announced faithfully through a performance built around the preacher’s status.

Corinth knew public competitions in rhetoric and the social influence eloquence could bring. Paul would not let those habits determine the church’s birth. He spoke intelligibly and earnestly, but his authority rested in what God had done in Christ.

wordNotes.explanation: The phrase denotes speech presented as superior or elevated according to admired rhetorical standards. Paul did not make verbal brilliance the proof of his message. He declared God’s testimony plainly enough that attention would rest on its subject rather than the speaker’s performance.

verses.bibleText: For I determined not to know any thing among you, save Jesus Christ, and him crucified.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
“I determined not to know any thing among you, save Jesus Christ, and him crucified” describes a governing resolve, not a refusal to discuss creation, ethics, resurrection, or the life of the church. The letter itself ranges across all those subjects. Paul means that every subject is ordered by the crucified Christ.

The last two words are indispensable. A teacher could speak admiringly of Jesus while avoiding the shame and claim of his death. Paul will not offer an inspiring Christ detached from atonement, self-giving love, and God’s judgment on human pride. The cross supplies both the message by which sinners are saved and the pattern by which Christian wisdom is recognized.

The verb “determined” indicates a considered judgment. Paul did not accidentally lack a broader program; he chose the center by which every part of his teaching would be governed. Acts 18 records that his Corinthian ministry included synagogue reasoning, house-based instruction, and an extended stay, all compatible with this resolve.

“Christ crucified” also resists a split between doctrine and ethics. The cross explains atonement, but it simultaneously reveals the form of divine love and apostolic service. Later instructions about foregoing rights, caring for weaker believers, and ordering gifts for another’s good will all grow from this knowledge.

To “know” Christ in this sense is more than possessing information. Paul’s ministry among them was shaped by allegiance to the one who died and rose. That center allowed no rival personality—including Paul—to become the church’s foundation.

wordNotes.explanation: Paul describes a settled ministerial resolve, not deliberate ignorance of every other subject. Among the Corinthians he would let the crucified Christ govern what he proclaimed and how he proclaimed it, refusing any approach that pushed the cross to the margins.

verses.bibleText: And I was with you in weakness, and in fear, and in much trembling.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul came “in weakness, and in fear, and in much trembling.” He offers no image of the apostle as an invulnerable hero. His weakness may have included bodily strain, the hostility surrounding the mission, and sober awareness of the task; the text does not isolate one cause. Acts records that the Lord encouraged him in Corinth not to be afraid, suggesting the danger was real.

Fear here is not unbelief masquerading as humility. Paul stayed, preached, worked, and endured opposition for many months. Courage is visible precisely because he acted while conscious of weakness.

Paul had recently encountered resistance in Macedonia and would face fresh opposition in Corinth. Acts records a vision in which the Lord tells him not to fear and promises continued protection while he speaks. That setting helps distinguish trembling from cowardice: the Lord addresses real vulnerability and enables perseverance within it.

Ancient audiences often expected an accomplished speaker to project confidence and control. Paul’s admission would not strengthen his image by those standards. He includes it because the gospel’s credibility cannot depend on maintaining the leader’s heroic persona. God’s strength becomes visible through a witness who remains obedient without pretending to be invulnerable.

That memory advances the argument of chapter 1. The Corinthians want leaders who embody impressive strength, but their founding apostle remembers trembling. God did not wait for Paul to feel commanding before using him. Christian ministry may require preparation and confidence in the gospel, yet it never outgrows dependence.

wordNotes.explanation: The paired expression conveys profound human frailty and sober awareness of responsibility, not unbelief in the gospel. Paul’s vulnerable presence denied Corinth a polished religious celebrity and made clearer that the lasting effect of his ministry came from God’s power.

verses.bibleText: And my speech and my preaching was not with enticing words of man's wisdom, but in demonstration of the Spirit and of power:

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul contrasts “enticing words of man’s wisdom” with “demonstration of the Spirit and of power.” A demonstration was proof that established a case. Here the decisive proof was not rhetorical manipulation but the Spirit’s work through the proclaimed gospel—awakening faith, changing lives, and creating a congregation where none had existed.

“Enticing” or persuasive words can refer neutrally to convincing argument, but the contrast gives the phrase a critical edge: persuasion located “in man’s wisdom” rather than the Spirit. Paul is alert to the possibility that people can be moved psychologically without being brought under truth.

The Spirit’s demonstration should be read with the conversion of Corinth in view. Jews and Gentiles abandoned old allegiances, confessed Jesus as Lord, and learned to gather across social lines. Miraculous gifts also appeared among them, yet chapters 12–14 will insist that manifestations are genuine demonstrations only when they confess Christ and serve the body.

Power is therefore recognized by gospel-shaped fruit as well as extraordinary events. This does not pit the Holy Spirit against careful explanation. Paul’s own letters are sustained arguments. The contrast concerns the source of persuasion and the object of trust. Techniques that bypass judgment, flatter an audience, or bind people to a charismatic speaker are incompatible with the freedom produced by the Spirit.

Nor does “power” require a spectacle at every sermon. God’s power is already identified with the message of the cross. Signs may accompany apostolic mission, but the lasting demonstration is that hearers come under Christ’s lordship through a word the surrounding culture calls weak.

wordNotes.term: Demonstration of the Spirit and of power
wordNotes.explanation: “Demonstration” describes convincing evidence, but Paul locates it in the Spirit’s effective work rather than rhetorical technique. The gospel authenticated itself by awakening faith and transforming lives, showing that divine power operated through a messenger who lacked outward impressiveness.

verses.bibleText: That your faith should not stand in the wisdom of men, but in the power of God.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul explains why his manner mattered: faith must stand “in the power of God,” not in human wisdom. A congregation created by fascination with a speaker remains dependent on that speaker’s appeal. When a more fashionable voice arrives, allegiance shifts. Corinth’s parties show how unstable such faith can become.

God’s power is not bare force. In this context it is saving power exercised through Christ crucified and applied by the Spirit. Faith rests securely when its ground is God’s act, promise, and character.

The noun translated “stand” conveys existence or being. Paul wants their faith to have its continuing reality in God’s power. The issue extends beyond the moment of conversion: what creates a congregation will also determine what it seeks to preserve it.

Reliance on human wisdom produces a church anxious about cultural approval and vulnerable to each new persuasive personality. Reliance on God’s power permits careful engagement with ideas without surrendering the gospel’s terms. Faith may be reasoned and tested, but its object is the God revealed in Christ, not the reasoner’s skill.

The verse therefore speaks to both preaching and hearing. Christian teachers should give reasons, use language well, and labor for clarity. Hearers should value such service. But neither should confuse the instrument with the foundation. The test of ministry is not whether people leave impressed by its brilliance; it is whether their confidence is transferred to God.

wordNotes.explanation: To “stand” is to rest upon a supporting foundation. Faith built on a personality’s brilliance remains vulnerable to a more impressive personality; faith grounded in God’s power depends on the saving reality of Christ and can endure when human reputation changes.

verses.bibleText: Howbeit we speak wisdom among them that are perfect: yet not the wisdom of this world, nor of the princes of this world, that come to nought:

commentary.detailedExplanation:
After rejecting the age’s wisdom, Paul insists that the gospel truly is wisdom “among them that are perfect”—that is, mature. Christian faith is not a celebration of mental shallowness. It contains a coherent account of God, creation, salvation, history, and the world’s end, but it becomes recognizable as wisdom only when the cross has reordered the hearer’s values.

“The princes of this world” may include political rulers and the spiritual powers working through the present age. Verse 8 particularly implicates those who crucified Jesus. However powerful they appear, they are “coming to nought.” Their judgments cannot define ultimate reality.

“Perfect” translates teleioi, mature or complete, and does not claim sinless perfection. In 3:1 Paul will deny that the Corinthians presently display such maturity. The mature recognize the cross as wisdom and let it govern relationships.

The “wisdom of this world” belongs to “this age,” the present order moving toward judgment. Its rulers are “coming to nought,” a verb that can mean being rendered ineffective or abolished. Their fading authority makes imitation shortsighted.

Christian wisdom therefore includes an eschatological estimate of power. It can respect civic office and learning while remembering that neither sets the terms of God’s future.

Paul’s phrase “we speak wisdom” also corrects any hearer who might use the previous paragraph to excuse shallow teaching. Pastors should help believers trace the coherence of revelation, wrestle with difficult texts, and grow in judgment. What must be rejected is not depth but the goal of using depth to establish rank. The mature can receive complex instruction because the cross has made them teachable.

Maturity therefore does not mean graduating from the cross to more sophisticated teaching. It means perceiving more fully the wisdom already concentrated there. The Corinthians’ love of prestige was not advanced spirituality; it was evidence that they had not yet learned how God’s wisdom looks.

wordNotes.explanation: “Perfect” here means mature or fully initiated into Christian understanding, not sinlessly complete. The mature recognize the cross as God’s wisdom and let it reshape their values. Corinthian rivalry shows that claims to advanced spirituality can coexist with serious immaturity.

verses.bibleText: But we speak the wisdom of God in a mystery, even the hidden wisdom, which God ordained before the world unto our glory:

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul calls the gospel “the wisdom of God in a mystery.” In his usage, a mystery is not a puzzle reserved for an inner circle. It is God’s long-hidden purpose now disclosed through Christ. Human investigation did not uncover it, yet God always intended to reveal it.

The Greek mystērion was used in several settings, including secret rites, but Paul’s own explanation controls its sense: something once hidden in God’s counsel and now made known. The mystery is publicly preached, not sold to initiates through degrees of access.

“Before the world” reaches behind the present age to God’s eternal purpose. “Unto our glory” looks forward to the other side of it. The cross stands at the center of that long horizon, joining divine purpose, present revelation, and future transformation.

Glory here cannot mean Corinthian self-display. It is the shared destiny of conformity to Christ, received by those whom the world may call foolish and weak.

That purpose was “ordained before the world unto our glory.” Salvation is no emergency repair after the cross surprised God. Before the ages, God purposed to bring his people into the glory bound up with Christ—restored character, resurrection life, and participation in the renewed creation.

The preposition “unto our glory” also forbids treating God’s glory and human good as competitors. God glorifies himself by bringing redeemed people into the likeness and inheritance of the Son. This promised glory includes moral transformation now and bodily resurrection later. It is not the private status Corinth seeks, because every member receives it in communion with Christ.

The verse humbles every claim to spiritual elitism. Believers know the mystery because God revealed it, not because they outperformed others intellectually. Its depth invites lifelong thought, while its origin keeps thought worshipful.

wordNotes.explanation: God’s saving purpose, formerly hidden and now disclosed in Christ. In Paul it is proclaimed openly; it is not secret teaching reserved for a spiritual elite.

verses.bibleText: Which none of the princes of this world knew: for had they known it, they would not have crucified the Lord of glory.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The rulers’ ignorance became visible when they “crucified the Lord of glory.” The title creates a deliberate collision: the one subjected to Rome’s most degrading execution was in truth the bearer of divine glory. Their sentence revealed how completely the age had misjudged both power and God.

Paul does not imply that fuller information alone would have produced saving faith. He shows that worldly wisdom, despite its confidence, failed at the decisive moment. Political calculation, religious anxiety, and imperial violence converged against the Messiah.

The “princes” may include Herod, Pilate, and the authorities involved in Jesus’ death, while Paul’s wider theology also recognizes spiritual powers operating through human structures. The text does not allow blame to be shifted onto one ethnic group. Roman execution and local leadership together represent the age’s opposition.

“Lord of glory” applies to the crucified Jesus a title of astonishing majesty. The phrase means that the cross was not simply human tragedy later reversed by God. The glorious Lord willingly entered the event through which divine wisdom would be accomplished.

Had the rulers truly understood, they would not have acted as they did. Their ignorance mitigates neither the evil nor the need for grace; it exposes the blindness of power separated from God.

Ignorance and responsibility coexist here. The rulers did not grasp God’s plan, yet Paul still names them as agents of the crucifixion. Scripture can recognize diminished understanding without turning it into innocence. This is important whenever institutions harm people through confident blindness: lack of self-awareness does not remove the need for truth, repentance, and judgment.

Human guilt and God’s purpose operate together without making God the author of evil. The rulers acted wickedly; through that very act God accomplished redemption. The cross thus exposes the powers and becomes the means of their defeat.

wordNotes.explanation: The title joins the crucified Jesus with divine glory in a startling contrast. Earthly rulers treated him as disposable, yet the one they executed possesses the glory they failed to discern. The phrase exposes both their ignorance and the majesty concealed in the cross.

verses.bibleText: But as it is written, Eye hath not seen, nor ear heard, neither have entered into the heart of man, the things which God hath prepared for them that love him.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
This verse celebrates God’s marvelous plan of salvation. “Eye hath not seen, nor ear heard” means that no human faculty—observation, testimony, reason, or imagination—could have discovered or designed what God purposed. Paul is not chiefly offering a poetic statement that heaven is too beautiful to describe. His subject from verse 6 onward is God’s hidden wisdom: the saving purpose revealed in Jesus Christ, “the Lord of glory,” who was crucified.

The cross makes the point concrete. Human wisdom would never have imagined the Creator rescuing sinners through the shameful death of His Son, turning apparent defeat into victory, forgiving His enemies, reconciling them to Himself, gathering one people in Christ, and promising resurrection life. The rulers’ decision to crucify Jesus in verse 8 displays the blindness described in verse 9. What human sight, report, and thought failed to recognize, God had already prepared.

Verse 10 must be read with verse 9: “But God hath revealed them unto us by his Spirit.” Paul is not saying that the plan of salvation remains unknowable. It lay beyond unaided human discovery, but the Spirit has now disclosed it through the gospel. Believers do not comprehend every depth of God, yet they are not left guessing about the center of His saving work. The Spirit reveals the meaning of the cross, awakens faith in Christ, and makes known “the things that are freely given to us of God” in verse 12.

Paul introduces the language with “as it is written.” The wording most closely recalls Isaiah 64:4 and belongs to Isaiah’s wider vision of God acting in unexpected ways and bringing His people into a renewed creation, although the complete sentence does not reproduce one Old Testament verse word for word. The central witness is clear: God acts for His people in ways fallen human wisdom does not anticipate. “Prepared” also reaches back to verse 7, where this wisdom was ordained before the world for our glory. Calvary was not an emergency response to a plan gone wrong; salvation flows from God’s eternal purpose.

Those who “love him” do not purchase this salvation by their love. Love is the answering allegiance of people who receive God’s grace and cling to Christ rather than boasting in their own knowledge. The verse therefore holds present revelation and future glory together. God has revealed the plan in the gospel now, while its complete result—resurrection, incorruption, fellowship with God, and the renewed creation—awaits Christ’s return. From the cross to the new creation, salvation surpasses what humanity could conceive, yet God has made its heart known through the Spirit.

wordNotes.term: The things which God hath prepared
wordNotes.explanation: The “things” are God’s marvelous saving purpose in Christ, planned beyond unaided human perception and reaching toward final glory. The next verse says the Spirit has revealed them; Paul is not restricting the promise to unknown heavenly scenery but celebrating salvation’s divinely disclosed plan.

verses.bibleText: But God hath revealed them unto us by his Spirit: for the Spirit searcheth all things, yea, the deep things of God.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
What eye, ear, and unaided imagination could not reach, “God hath revealed ... by his Spirit.” Christian knowledge begins in divine self-disclosure. The Spirit does not add religious feeling to conclusions constructed independently; he makes known the saving purpose accomplished in Christ.

“Unto us” refers first to the apostolic witnesses through whom that gospel is communicated. The Corinthians know God’s wisdom because it was revealed and proclaimed, not because each believer possesses a private channel of infallible information. The same Spirit enables the church to understand the apostolic word, but personal impressions remain accountable to that public testimony.

The Spirit “searcheth all things, yea, the deep things of God.” Searching does not mean that he investigates because something is hidden from him. It expresses complete and active knowledge. Nothing in God lies outside the Spirit’s access, so his witness is competent. The chapter has a quietly Trinitarian shape: God prepared his wisdom, the Lord of glory was crucified, and the Spirit discloses what God has done.

This full divine knowledge gives Christian proclamation confidence. The apostles were not assembling clues into a merely probable account of salvation. God’s own Spirit taught them. Human interpreters remain limited and can misunderstand, which is why the church compares Scripture with Scripture, attends to language and context, and listens across the body. The limitation belongs to readers, not to God’s act of revelation.

Revealed truth can still be deep truth. The Spirit’s gift does not remove the need for patient thought, prayer, obedience, and communal discernment. A person may understand the wording of the cross while resisting its implications for pride and fellowship. Learning continues as the Spirit brings the received word to bear on the whole life.

The verse therefore supports neither skeptical uncertainty nor boastful claims to exhaustive insight. Believers know truly because God has spoken, and they remain humble because the depths belong to God. The Spirit gives enough light for faith and obedience while continually drawing the church further into the wisdom centered on Christ.

wordNotes.explanation: The “deep things” are the profound purposes known fully within God’s own life, especially the saving wisdom displayed in Christ. The Spirit does not discover them as an outsider; because he searches and knows them, he is able to reveal them truthfully to God’s people.

verses.bibleText: For what man knoweth the things of a man, save the spirit of man which is in him? even so the things of God knoweth no man, but the Spirit of God.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul uses an analogy from self-knowledge. Another person can observe words and actions, but the inner life is known most directly by the person’s own spirit. In a far greater way, “the Spirit of God” knows what belongs to God.

The analogy distinguishes the Holy Spirit from the human mind; it does not reduce him to an impersonal faculty. Every analogy has limits: a human spirit belongs to the person, while the Holy Spirit personally searches, reveals, and teaches with full knowledge of God. Paul’s comparison concerns access to inward reality, not the manner of divine existence.

“No man knoweth” also warns against claims that bypass revelation. Religious imagination can produce confident pictures of God drawn from desire, politics, or fear. The Spirit’s witness, given through the apostolic gospel and Scripture, corrects those projections.

The analogy also rules out the idea that created spirits, ancestors, or occult techniques can provide privileged access to God’s counsel. Knowledge of God comes from God’s own Spirit. Seeking hidden guidance through rival spiritual powers contradicts the source Paul identifies and exposes the seeker to deception.

Paul’s “things of God” includes both God’s nature and saving intention. The analogy supports access, not identity: as self-knowledge is inward, the Spirit’s knowledge is proper to God. Early Christian confession drew on such texts to speak of the Spirit’s full deity. A created messenger could report what he had been told; the Spirit searches and knows God’s depths.

wordNotes.explanation: Paul reasons from a limited analogy: a person’s inner life is known from within, and God’s purposes are known by God’s Spirit. The comparison emphasizes the Spirit’s intimate, adequate knowledge without reducing him to a mere human faculty or impersonal influence.

verses.bibleText: Now we have received, not the spirit of the world, but the spirit which is of God; that we might know the things that are freely given to us of God.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Believers have received “not the spirit of the world, but the spirit which is of God.” The contrast concerns two governing orientations. The world’s spirit prizes the status and self-sufficiency exposed in chapter 1; the Holy Spirit teaches grateful recognition of grace.

His purpose is “that we might know the things that are freely given to us of God.” The gospel’s gifts—Christ, forgiveness, holiness, adoption, resurrection hope—are not achievements waiting for the clever. The Spirit enables them to be understood as gifts.

“Received” is conversion language as well as instruction. The Spirit’s presence marks a transfer of rule away from the world’s values. Yet Corinth shows that old habits can continue to resist that new rule; reception calls for an ongoing learning of grace.

The verb “know” involves recognition and appreciation. A person may repeat that salvation is free while organizing church life around achievement and rank. The Spirit enables believers to see gifts as gifts, making gratitude and generosity possible.

Paul’s plural “we” again places knowledge within a community. The Spirit is not granted to create private elites but to form a people able to confess and share what God has freely given.

“Spirit of the world” need not name a second personal spirit equal to the Holy Spirit. It is the outlook of the age, though elsewhere Paul recognizes the personal evil powers that energize rebellion. The contrast remains asymmetrical: the Creator’s Spirit graciously gives understanding; the world’s pattern is passing away.

The Spirit enables knowledge of “things” in the plural, so grace is not reduced to a single conversion moment. The gifts include wisdom, righteousness, sanctification, redemption, adoption, the body’s diverse ministries, and resurrection hope. Their unity in Christ protects against congregations choosing one benefit as the whole gospel.

This knowledge is neither merely academic nor a private impression. It produces worship, obedience, and a new estimate of what is valuable. A church may speak frequently about spiritual things while still thinking with the world’s spirit. The sure sign of the Spirit’s instruction is that Christ’s grace, rather than human distinction, becomes central.

wordNotes.term: Freely given to us of God
wordNotes.explanation: The Spirit enables believers to recognize salvation as gift. “Freely given” shares the language of grace and excludes spiritual pride: insight into God’s purpose is not an achievement of an intellectual elite but a gracious disclosure received from him.

verses.bibleText: Which things also we speak, not in the words which man's wisdom teacheth, but which the Holy Ghost teacheth; comparing spiritual things with spiritual.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The gospel was not a human construction later decorated with religious language. Paul says that both the realities he proclaimed and the words in which he taught them stood under the Holy Spirit’s instruction. The Spirit who knows the depths of God made the saving purpose intelligible and guided its apostolic communication.

This is an important statement about inspiration. Paul does not describe mechanical dictation, and his own vocabulary, argument, and pastoral setting remain plainly visible. Yet those human features do not make the message merely human. Through the apostle’s real acts of thinking and writing, the Spirit communicated trustworthy truth. Attention to wording therefore belongs to faithful reading rather than verbal pedantry.

The closing phrase, translated “comparing spiritual things with spiritual,” is grammatically open to several closely related senses. It may refer to expressing spiritual truths in Spirit-taught words, interpreting spiritual realities by other spiritual realities, or explaining them to spiritual people. The wording does not let us choose one meaning with certainty. Each reading, however, keeps the same center: the source, fitting expression, and reception of the gospel depend on the Spirit rather than on the standards of fashionable rhetoric.

Spirit-taught speech is not careless speech. Revelation must be expressed, translated, reasoned through, and heard in context. Because teachers serve a message they received, they are obliged to handle language honestly, compare Scripture with Scripture, and make the apostolic meaning clear. Preparation and prayer belong together.

The contrast in this verse is therefore not between divine truth and serious study. It is between speech ruled by self-display and speech disciplined by what God has revealed. Christian teaching is faithful when its language serves the saving realities freely given in Christ and allows the cross, rather than the speaker’s reputation, to command attention.

wordNotes.explanation: The phrase can describe expressing Spirit-given realities in Spirit-taught words or interpreting spiritual matters for spiritual people. Either way, Paul’s point is dependence: the message and its fitting expression arise under the Spirit’s instruction, not from autonomous human wisdom.

verses.bibleText: But the natural man receiveth not the things of the Spirit of God: for they are foolishness unto him: neither can he know them, because they are spiritually discerned.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The “natural man” is the person considered apart from the renewing work of the Spirit. The Greek adjective points to life governed by merely human capacities and desires. Paul is not dividing humanity into intelligent and unintelligent people, nor is he condemning the created body.

Such a person rejects the Spirit’s things as foolish because their value cannot be judged by the age’s assumptions. “Spiritually discerned” does not mean accepted without evidence or protected from examination. It means that the Spirit must correct the heart’s resistance and teach the standards by which the cross is recognized as God’s wisdom.

Psychikos, translated “natural,” derives from psychē, ordinary creaturely life. In Jude 19 the same adjective describes people “having not the Spirit.” It should not be turned into a claim that emotions, psychology, or bodily existence are inherently evil.

“Foolishness unto him” links back to 1:18. The obstacle is not simply lack of data. The cross judges the self that wants to remain autonomous, so rejection carries a moral dimension. The Spirit’s work does not bypass evidence; he enables a truthful response to the evidence God has given.

Spiritual discernment remains open to correction because the Spirit who illumines also speaks consistently through Scripture. The word “receive” can mean welcome as well as understand. The natural person’s problem is not an IQ barrier but refusal of a gift that judges pride. This explains why patient argument and loving witness matter without imagining that they alone can create faith. Christians reason and testify while praying for the Spirit to make the heart willing to welcome the truth.

Discernment is necessary because the Spirit’s things may initially threaten settled identities and interests. The natural person can admire Christian ethics selectively while resisting the Lord who claims the self. The Spirit joins understanding with repentance. This is why moral posture, communal practice, and intellectual interpretation cannot finally be separated.

Sin affects reason morally, bending it toward self-rule. Conversion does not end thinking; it liberates thought to receive revelation, test claims by Scripture, and confess truths pride once dismissed.

wordNotes.explanation: Life limited to ordinary human capacities and lacking the Holy Spirit’s renewing direction. The term does not make the created body or careful reasoning evil.

verses.bibleText: But he that is spiritual judgeth all things, yet he himself is judged of no man.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The spiritual person “judgeth all things”: guided by the Spirit, he or she can assess claims according to God’s revealed wisdom. The statement is about discernment, not omniscience. It gives no believer permission to pronounce on every matter without study or to treat private impressions as infallible.

“Yet he himself is judged of no man” means that the world, while operating with standards overturned by the cross, cannot render a competent verdict on Spirit-shaped life. It does not place Christians beyond correction, civil responsibility, or the judgment of the church. Paul is about to correct these very “spiritual” Corinthians.

The verb for “judgeth” is the same family used for spiritual discernment. It means examine or appraise. The Spirit-shaped person can recognize the direction in which teachings and practices move because the cross supplies a new standard.

Paul himself demonstrates this activity throughout the letter: he evaluates slogans, moral conduct, worship, and teaching. His judgments use reasons and scriptural arguments, not appeals to untouchable intuition.

The world’s inability to assess such a person is qualified by context. It cannot appreciate the wisdom it calls foolish. Other believers can and must test conduct; even a spiritually mature person remains accountable to the Lord and the body.

Discernment “of all things” is comprehensive in principle rather than exhaustive in information. The Spirit gives a framework capable of testing every area of life under Christ, not instant expertise in medicine, law, history, or another person’s motives. Mature believers respect created knowledge and consult people with relevant skill while refusing any expertise the right to overrule God’s moral word.

Paul does not say the spiritual person is never misunderstood. “Judged of no man” concerns the competence of the Spirit-less standard, not the absence of social consequences. The apostles are condemned by courts and audiences while remaining vindicated before God. Discernment may therefore carry costly nonconformity without becoming contempt for every outsider.

True discernment remains teachable. Because it comes from the Spirit, it produces the humility, holiness, and love seen in Christ rather than an insulated sense of superiority.

wordNotes.explanation: Here “judgeth” means discerns or evaluates. The Spirit-enabled person can assess claims by God’s revealed wisdom, especially the cross. This is not permission for arrogant criticism or immunity from accountability; the following chapters condemn precisely such pride and premature judgment.

verses.bibleText: For who hath known the mind of the Lord, that he may instruct him? But we have the mind of Christ.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The question from Isaiah—“Who hath known the mind of the Lord, that he may instruct him?”—ends every attempt to place God in the pupil’s chair. No creature audits God’s wisdom or supplies what he lacks.

The quotation comes from Isaiah 40:13, where God’s incomparable wisdom comforts exiles: no counselor instructed the Creator, and no empire exceeds his power. Paul applies that confession to the revelation given in Christ.

“Mind of Christ” includes Christ’s understanding but, in the next chapter and again in Philippians 2, it is inseparable from his humble pattern. A mind that can recite theology while cultivating envy has not yet become recognizably Christ’s.

The plural claim also protects against solitary certainty. The church discerns together under the apostolic word. A person announcing “I have the mind of Christ” to end discussion is using the phrase in the opposite spirit from Paul’s argument.

Paul then makes the astonishing claim, “we have the mind of Christ.” The plural matters. He is not celebrating a private stream of superior insight but the understanding shared by the apostolic community through the Spirit. To possess Christ’s mind is to learn reality from the crucified Lord: humility instead of status, service instead of self-display, and confidence in God’s revealed purpose.

Paul’s answer “we” follows a question expecting “no one.” No creature instructed God; nevertheless, God has shared Christ’s mind by revelation. The claim is therefore entirely gracious. It produces confidence in what God has spoken and modesty about everything believers have inferred. Where certainty exceeds the revealed word, the language of Christ’s mind should not be used to disguise speculation.

This claim is accountable to the gospel Paul has proclaimed. It cannot sanctify every Christian opinion. The mind of Christ is recognized where Scripture’s witness is received and the character of the cross begins to shape the church.

wordNotes.explanation: To possess Christ’s mind is to share, through the Spirit, the pattern of judgment revealed in the crucified Lord. It is a corporate gift that teaches the church to value humility, self-giving love, and divine wisdom rather than a claim that any individual knows everything.
